quoteFirst: My Father and Mother, sister Harriet, Mary Bridges, and Uncle William Stevens accompanied us as far as Boston. We all dined at Wildes’ Hotel, 11 Elm Street, then we bade our friends good-bye and went to the Station, and at three o’clock left for Providence, arrived there
quoteLast: , and went directly on board a Steam Ferry boat and crossed Narragansett Bay, and then took cars for Stonington, Conn., where we arrived about nine o’clock, and went on board the Steamboat Lexington for New York.
title: A sketch of the early days of the woolen industry in North Andover, Massachusetts
author: Stevens, Nathaniel, 1857-1946

quoteFirst: ST. OMER and WATTEN to AUDRUICQ, arriving there
quoteLast: Here we found lurries waiting to take us to our new billeting area, AUDREHEM, a pleasant village just big enough to hold us.
title:
The War History of the 1st/4th Battalion, the Loyal North Lancashire Regiment
now the Loyal Regiment (North Lancashire), 1914-1918
author: Anonymous

quoteFirst: I suddenly bethought me that I might possibly gain news of him at the café des Variétés, which he frequented most days; also, a month previous to this time, he had had a quarrel with Soulié, which had ended in the exchange of a couple of pistol shots. It was now
quoteTime: nearly five o'clock in the afternoon
quoteLast: . Rochefort (a friend of mine, a clever fellow who composed several original plays, one of them being _Jocko_, besides some delightful poems) was taking a glass of absinthe at one of the café tables. He rose when he caught sight of me.
title: My Memoirs, Vol. IV, 1830 to 1831
author: Dumas, Alexandre, 1802-1870; Waller, E. M. (Emily Mary) [Translator]

quoteLast: I will have tarts and jellies, and a gallon bowl of punch; at ten, a hot supper of two dishes. If I am in a good humour, and Grace _behaves herself_, she shall sit down with me.
title: A history of English lotteries
author: Ashton, John, 1834-1911

title: The Living Museum, Vol. XV No. 6, October 1953
author: Various; Eifert, Virginia S. (Virginia Snider), 1911-1966 [Editor]

quoteTime: About five of the clock in the afternoon
quoteLast: they rode again to court, to hear service in the King’s chapel, keeping the same order they did at their return from thence in the morning, every knight riding between his two esquires, and his page following him.
title: The works of Thomas Middleton, Volume 5 (of 5)
author: Middleton, Thomas, -1627; Dekker, Thomas, 1572?-1632; Rowley, William, 1585?-1642?; Dyce, Alexander, 1798-1869 [Editor]

quoteFirst: As we are in London ‘in the season,’ in the Park,
quoteLast: , we see all the ‘swelldom’ driving and riding, for it is here they take their airing. The Park itself is lovely, with large, perfect roads and walks, grand, magnificent old trees, plump, clean sheep and graceful deer grazing contentedly, as well they may in such quarters.
title: A Bundle of Letters from over the Sea
author: Robinson, Louise B.

quoteFirst: When I told him of the forelooper and the oxen he said he would go at once and follow their spoor, otherwise we should never see them again, and asked for a little brandy to wash his mouth out, for he, as well as myself, could not articulate plainly, and then started after them
quoteLast: I was again left alone to pass another sleepless night, and the fifth day without water or food.  I frequently rinsed my mouth out with brandy, which kept my tongue from swelling.
title: Twenty-Five Years in a Waggon in South Africa: Sport and Travel in South Africa
author: Anderson, Andrew A.

quoteFirst: Immediately a fierce firing commenced, and they said the rebels were flying off to the Fyzabad road. We commenced shelling them: there was a tremendous day's firing, notwithstanding, and the troops had a fearful day's work.
title: A Diary Kept by Mrs. R. C. Germon, at Lucknow, Between the Months of May and December, 1857
author: Germon, Maria

quoteFirst: The Fifth Corps got to Jettersville
quoteLast: , and I immediately intrenched it across the Burkeville road with the determination to stay there till the main army could come up, for I hoped we could force Lee to surrender at Amelia Court House, since a firm hold on Jettersville would cut him off from his line of retreat toward Burkeville.
title: Personal Memoirs of P. H. Sheridan, Volume 2, Part 5
author: Sheridan, Philip Henry, 1831-1888
